title: Läuschen und Flöhchen
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 30. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The thirtieth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 30), a tiny cumulative chain-rhyme in the vein of “The House That Jack Built.” A little louse and a little flea keep house together; when the louse burns to death in the beer-brewing, the flea’s weeping sets off a chain in which the door, the broom, the cart, the dunghill, the tree, the girl and at last the spring each ask the one before “why do you do so?” and answer by heaping on grief, until the spring’s flood drowns them all. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as fieng, daß, Stubenthüre and Thürchen, and a swarm of -chen/-lein diminutives.

sentences.t: Ein Läuschen und ein Flöhchen die lebten zusammen in einem Haushalte und brauten das Bier in einer Eierschale.
sentences.l: A little-louse and a little-flea, they lived together in a household and brewed the beer in an eggshell.
sentences.i: A little louse and a little flea lived together and kept house, and were brewing beer in an eggshell.
sentences.n: The whole tale runs on -chen/-lein diminutives — everything is tiny and endearing: Läuschen (little louse), Flöhchen (little flea), and soon Thürchen, Besenchen, Wägelchen, Mistchen, Bäumchen, Brünnchen. Note the resumptive “die” echoing the subject before the verb (“Ein Läuschen und ein Flöhchen die lebten”); Grimm often points back to the subject with a demonstrative.

sentences.t: Da fiel das Läuschen hinein und verbrannte sich.
sentences.l: There the little-louse fell in and burnt itself.
sentences.i: Then the little louse fell in and burnt to death.
sentences.n: “verbrannte sich” — the reflexive sich verbrennen, to burn oneself; here fatally.

sentences.t: Darüber fieng das Flöhchen an laut zu schreien.
sentences.l: Over that the little-flea began to cry loudly.
sentences.i: At this the little flea began to wail aloud.
sentences.n: “fieng ... an” is the separable verb anfangen (to begin); the prefix an- jumps to the end of the clause — you will see “fieng an” again and again in this tale. “fieng” is the 1857 spelling of fing.

sentences.t: Da sprach die kleine Stubenthüre „was schreist du, Flöhchen?“ „Weil Läuschen sich verbrannt hat.“
sentences.l: Then the little parlour-door spoke, „what are you crying for, little-flea?“ „Because little-louse has burnt herself.“
sentences.i: Then the little parlour door said, ‘Why are you crying, little flea?’ ‘Because the little louse has burnt herself.’
sentences.n: Here the cumulative chain begins that drives the whole tale: each thing asks the one before it (“was … du?”, why do you do that?), and the answer heaps on one more line of the growing refrain. Stubenthüre is the 1857 spelling (Thüre for Tür); the Stube is the parlour.

sentences.t: Da fieng das Thürchen an zu knarren.
sentences.l: Then the little-door began to creak.

sentences.t: Da sprach ein Besenchen in der Ecke „was knarrst du, Thürchen?“ „Soll ich nicht knarren?
sentences.l: Then a little-broom in the corner spoke, „what are you creaking for, little-door?“ „Should I not creak?
sentences.i: Then a little broom in the corner said, ‘Why are you creaking, little door?’ ‘Should I not creak?
sentences.n: The other half of the pattern: each creature answers “Soll ich nicht …?” (“Should I not …?”) and then recites the whole chain so far. The prose runs straight on into the verse refrain that follows.

sentences.t:
Läuschen hat sich verbrannt,
Flöhchen weint.“
sentences.l:
Little-louse has burnt herself,
little-flea weeps.“
sentences.n: The refrain grows by one line each round — a cumulative chain-rhyme, like “The House That Jack Built.” Watch it lengthen with each new character: burnt, weeps, creaks, sweeps, runs, burns, shakes.

sentences.t: Da fieng das Besenchen an entsetzlich zu kehren.
sentences.l: Then the little-broom began to sweep dreadfully.
sentences.i: Then the little broom began to sweep like mad.
sentences.n: “entsetzlich” (dreadfully, terribly) is the tale’s running intensifier — the broom sweeps, the cart runs, the dunghill burns and the spring flows, all “entsetzlich.”

sentences.t: Da kam ein Wägelchen vorbei und sprach „was kehrst du, Besenchen?“ „Soll ich nicht kehren?
sentences.l: Then a little-cart came by and spoke, „what are you sweeping for, little-broom?“ „Should I not sweep?
sentences.i: Then a little cart came by and said, ‘Why are you sweeping, little broom?’ ‘Should I not sweep?

sentences.t:
Läuschen hat sich verbrannt,
Flöhchen weint,
Thürchen knarrt.“
sentences.l:
Little-louse has burnt herself,
little-flea weeps,
little-door creaks.“

sentences.t: Da sprach das Wägelchen „so will ich rennen,“ und fieng an entsetzlich zu rennen.
sentences.l: Then the little-cart spoke, „so I will run,“ and began to run dreadfully.
sentences.i: Then the little cart said, ‘Then I will run,’ and set off running like mad.
sentences.n: “so will ich …” — here “so” = “then, in that case,” and “will” is wollen (to want, to intend), not the English future “will.” From now on each thing vows to join in.

sentences.t: Da sprach das Mistchen, an dem es vorbei rannte, „was rennst du, Wägelchen?“ „Soll ich nicht rennen?
sentences.l: Then the little-dung-heap, past which it ran, spoke, „what are you running for, little-cart?“ „Should I not run?
sentences.i: Then the little dunghill it ran past said, ‘Why are you running, little cart?’ ‘Should I not run?
sentences.n: “an dem es vorbei rannte” — literally “past which it ran”; the relative “an dem” (at/past which) points back to the Mistchen. Mistchen is the diminutive of Mist (dung, manure) — the little dunghill.

sentences.t:
Läuschen hat sich verbrannt,
Flöhchen weint,
Thürchen knarrt,
Besenchen kehrt.“
sentences.l:
Little-louse has burnt herself,
little-flea weeps,
little-door creaks,
little-broom sweeps.“

sentences.t: Da sprach das Mistchen „so will ich entsetzlich brennen,“ und fieng an in hellem Feuer zu brennen.
sentences.l: Then the little-dung-heap spoke, „so I will burn dreadfully,“ and began to burn in bright fire.
sentences.i: Then the little dunghill said, ‘Then I will burn dreadfully,’ and burst into bright flame.

sentences.t: Da stand ein Bäumchen neben dem Mistchen, das sprach „Mistchen, warum brennst du?“ „Soll ich nicht brennen?
sentences.l: There stood a little-tree beside the little-dung-heap, which spoke, „little-dung-heap, why do you burn?“ „Should I not burn?
sentences.i: A little tree stood beside the dunghill, and it said, ‘Little dunghill, why are you burning?’ ‘Should I not burn?
sentences.n: “das sprach” — “das” is a demonstrative/relative pointing back to the neuter Bäumchen (which spoke). From here the question shifts to “warum …?” (why?) alongside the earlier “was …?”

sentences.t:
Läuschen hat sich verbrannt,
Flöhchen weint,
Thürchen knarrt,
Besenchen kehrt,
Wägelchen rennt.“
sentences.l:
Little-louse has burnt herself,
little-flea weeps,
little-door creaks,
little-broom sweeps,
little-cart runs.“

sentences.t: Da sprach das Bäumchen „so will ich mich schütteln,“ und fieng an sich zu schütteln, daß all seine Blätter abfielen.
sentences.l: Then the little-tree spoke, „so I will shake myself,“ and began to shake itself, so that all its leaves fell off.
sentences.i: Then the little tree said, ‘Then I will shake myself,’ and began to shake itself until all its leaves fell off.
sentences.n: “sich schütteln” = to shake oneself (reflexive: mich, sich). “abfielen” is abfallen (to fall off), a separable verb, here in the preterite. “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Das sah ein Mädchen, das mit seinem Wasserkrügelchen heran kam und sprach „Bäumchen, was schüttelst du dich?“ „Soll ich mich nicht schütteln?
sentences.l: This saw a girl, who came up with her little-water-jug and spoke, „little-tree, what are you shaking yourself for?“ „Should I not shake myself?
sentences.i: A girl saw this, who came up with her little water-jug and said, ‘Little tree, why are you shaking yourself?’ ‘Should I not shake myself?
sentences.n: “seinem” = “its/her”: das Mädchen is grammatically neuter, so the possessive is “sein” even though the girl is female. “heran kam” = herankommen, to come up/near (separable).

sentences.t:
Läuschen hat sich verbrannt,
Flöhchen weint,
Thürchen knarrt,
Besenchen kehrt,
Wägelchen rennt,
Mistchen brennt.“
sentences.l:
Little-louse has burnt herself,
little-flea weeps,
little-door creaks,
little-broom sweeps,
little-cart runs,
little-dung-heap burns.“

sentences.t: Da sprach das Mädchen „so will ich mein Wasserkrügelchen zerbrechen,“ und zerbrach das Wasserkrügelchen.
sentences.l: Then the girl spoke, „so I will break my little-water-jug,“ and broke the little-water-jug.
sentences.i: Then the girl said, ‘Then I will break my little water-jug,’ and broke the little water-jug to pieces.
sentences.n: “zerbrechen” — the prefix zer- means to pieces / apart, so zerbrechen = to shatter, break in pieces (preterite zerbrach).

sentences.t: Da sprach das Brünnlein, aus dem das Wasser quoll, „Mädchen, was zerbrichst du dein Wasserkrügelchen?“ „Soll ich mein Wasserkrügelchen nicht zerbrechen?
sentences.l: Then the little-spring, out of which the water welled, spoke, „girl, what are you breaking your little-water-jug for?“ „Should I not break my little-water-jug?
sentences.i: Then the little spring the water welled from said, ‘Girl, why are you breaking your little water-jug?’ ‘Should I not break my little water-jug?
sentences.n: “Brünnlein” here and “Brünnchen” below are two diminutives of Brunnen (spring, well) — Grimm uses both in the same passage. “quoll” is the preterite of quellen (to well up, gush).

sentences.t:
Läuschen hat sich verbrannt,
Flöhchen weint,
Thürchen knarrt,
Besenchen kehrt,
Wägelchen rennt,
Mistchen brennt,
Bäumchen schüttelt sich.“
sentences.l:
Little-louse has burnt herself,
little-flea weeps,
little-door creaks,
little-broom sweeps,
little-cart runs,
little-dung-heap burns,
little-tree shakes itself.“

sentences.t: „Ei,“ sagte das Brünnchen, „so will ich anfangen zu fließen,“ und fieng an entsetzlich zu fließen.
sentences.l: „Ah,“ said the little-spring, „so I will begin to flow,“ and began to flow dreadfully.
sentences.i: ‘Well then,’ said the little spring, ‘I will start to flow,’ and began to flow dreadfully.
sentences.n: “Ei” is an old exclamation — of surprise or resignation, roughly “Well!” or “Oh!” “anfangen zu fließen” and “fieng an … zu fließen” are the same verb (anfangen, to begin) twice in one sentence.

sentences.t: Und in dem Wasser ist alles ertrunken, das Mädchen, das Bäumchen, das Mistchen, das Wägelchen, das Besenchen, das Thürchen, das Flöhchen, das Läuschen, alles miteinander.
sentences.l: And in the water everything drowned, the girl, the little-tree, the little-dung-heap, the little-cart, the little-broom, the little-door, the little-flea, the little-louse, all together.
sentences.i: And in the water everything was drowned — the girl, the little tree, the dunghill, the cart, the broom, the door, the flea, the louse, all of them together.
sentences.n: The tale closes by running the whole chain backwards, drowning each character in reverse. “ist … ertrunken” is the perfect tense; ertrinken (to drown) takes sein, not haben, because it is a change of state. “alles miteinander” = “all together, every last one.”
